question: State the definitions of compactness and sequential compactness for a metric space (X, d).
expectedPoints: A set K ⊆ X is compact if every open cover of K has a finite subcover.
A set K ⊆ X is sequentially compact if every sequence in K has a convergent subsequence whose limit is in K.

question: Explain, with a proof outline, why in a metric space (X, d), a set K is compact if and only if it is sequentially compact. Your answer should identify the key steps in the proof of each implication.
expectedPoints: (Compact ⇒ Seq. Compact): Use total boundedness and the fact that an infinite set in a compact space has a limit point. Construct a subsequence converging to that limit point.

(Seq. Compact ⇒ Compact): 1. Show that sequential compactness implies total boundedness (by contradiction). 2. Show that sequential compactness implies the space is complete (Cauchy sequences have convergent subsequences). 3. Use the result that a totally bounded and complete space is compact (via the existence of a finite ε-net for every ε > 0).

question: Prove in detail that if a subset K of a metric space (X, d) is compact, then it is sequentially compact. Your proof must clearly show how the property of compactness is used to extract a convergent subsequence from any given sequence.
expectedPoints: Assume K is compact. Let (x_n) be a sequence in K. Consider two cases: whether the set {x_n} is finite or infinite.
Case 1 (Finite): Some value is repeated infinitely often, providing a constant, convergent subsequence.
Case 2 (Infinite): Since K is compact, it is limit point compact. The infinite set {x_n} has a limit point p in K.
Construct a subsequence (x_{n_k}) converging to p: For each k, choose n_k > n_{k-1} such that x_{n_k} ∈ B(p, 1/k). This is possible because p is a limit point.
Conclude that this constructed subsequence converges to p, which is in K.
